chunk_summary: Pediatric metabolic risk in India involves early-onset cardiometabolic complications, influenced by central adiposity and unique BMI thresholds. IDF pediatric criteria guide risk assessment, emphasizing prevention of type 2 diabetes, ASCVD, and liver disease progression.
learning_objectives: Understand the clinical definition and components of pediatric metabolic risk, including central adiposity, dyslipidemia, and insulin resistance.
Recognize the unique metabolic risk profile of South Asian children, including lower BMI thresholds for complications.
Apply IDF pediatric criteria for metabolic syndrome in children aged 10-16 years and address risks in younger children.
Develop strategies to prevent youth-onset type 2 diabetes, ASCVD, and MASLD progression.
key_facts: South Asian children may develop metabolic complications at lower BMI compared to Western cohorts.
IDF pediatric criteria for ages 10-16 require central obesity (waist ≥90th percentile) plus at least two of: triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL, HDL-C <40 mg/dL, BP ≥130/85 mmHg, fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL.
MASLD (formerly NAFLD) is a key hepatic risk associated with pediatric metabolic syndrome.
Double burden in India: early undernutrition or rapid catch-up growth can lead to later central adiposity and amplified metabolic risks.
Abdominal obesity and any abnormal risk marker warrant intervention, even in children under age 10.
reference_quote: The IDF pediatric criteria (ages 10 to <16 years) require central obesity (waist ≥90th percentile) plus at least two of triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL, HDL-C <40 mg/dL, BP ≥130/85 mmHg, and fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL.
document_key_topics: Double burden of malnutrition in India
Prevention of youth-onset type 2 diabetes and MASLD
